pullRequest.title: [DOM-75231|DOM-75232] Filters, sorting and pagination info for PAT's GET endpoints

nodes.body: By default it will sort by Mongo ID, which is equivalent to creation date, right?
Personally I like more the `sortBy` naming than the `sortField`, but that is as opinionated as it comes.

To be honest, I'm completely against handling expiringSoon as a status. 

I expect the API to return `active` if the token is active, and when I filter tokens (once we have filters) I expect `?status=active` to include those expiring soon. Considering that "soon" is a criteria defined by my  administrator, so it may be a month, I want to still be able to interact with that token as if it is functional. 

Instead, I would add another field on the response, so that it is:

```json
{
  "name": "my-token",
  // ...
  "status": "active",
  "expiringSoon": true
} 
```

----

Comment update: Realized my comment does not apply, as we are not even including it in the response.

The status enum apparently only applies to the filter, which ends up relating to this:

```
      case PatStatus.Active => BsonDocument("isValid" -> true)
      case PatStatus.ExpiringSoon => BsonDocument("isValid" -> true, "expiresAt" -> BsonDocument("$gt" -> nowBson, "$lte" -> expiringSoonLimit))
```

My bad. I see now that you mapped `active` to `isValid` and `expiringSoon` also to valid. 
We definitively will need to document it, but as the actual field in the response is called `isActive` and not status, it is not _that_ conflicting.

Yes, I know this is kinda confusing, but this Statuses are the combination of both fields that are required by the UI. This will be part of the docs that we are going to create for PATs, but I haven't added this as a comment because I thought that the conditions were self-explained and if I added this as a comment in a different part of the code where the enum is define and then the conditions are changed because a future requirement the one doing that was never going to remember to change the comment in the other place and that's where everything starts falling down haha. But as for now the status align with these conditions:
  `active` - Valid token to be used.
  `expiringSoon` - Valid token to be used but its expiration time is in less than 7 days.
  `expired` - Already expired token.
  `revoked` - Token that has been revoked and is no longer valid, despite its expiration time.
I think these two may be leftover from the old name.

_⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟠 Major_

<details>
<summary>🧩 Analysis chain</summary>

🌐 Web query:

`In Scala `Enumeration`, what exception does `withName` throw for an unknown value, and what is the safe pattern for parsing untrusted input?`

💡 Result:

In Scala Enumeration, the withName method throws java.util.NoSuchElementException when no value matches the given name. This is because it internally performs values.find(_.toString == s).get, and .get on None throws this exception. The safe pattern for parsing untrusted input is to use values.find(_.toString == s) which returns Option[Value]. You can add a custom method to the enumeration object, such as: def withNameOption(name: String): Option[Value] = values.find(_.toString == name) Then handle the Option appropriately, e.g., withNameOption(input).getOrElse(defaultValue) or pattern match it. This avoids exceptions and is efficient since values is a fixed set.

Citations:

- 1: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/33593525/scala-safe-way-of-converting-string-to-enumeration-value
- 2: https://www.scala-lang.org/api/current/scala/Enumeration.html
- 3: https://github.com/scala/scala/blob/v2.12.8/src/library/scala/Enumeration.scala
- 4: https://nightly.scala-lang.org/api/scala/Enumeration.html
- 5: https://xuwei-k.github.io/scala-library-sxr/scala-library-2.10.0/scala/Enumeration.scala.html
- 6: https://www.scala-lang.org/api/3.3.1/scala/Enumeration.html
- 7: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/23117681/how-to-read-string-into-enumeration-without-throwing-an-exception
- 8: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/22589426/check-if-certain-string-exist-in-my-enum-values-without-nosuchelement-exception

</details>

**Safely parse query enum parameters before passing to service layer.**

`status`, `sortBy`, and `sortOrder` arrive as query strings and are converted with `.withName()` directly at lines 48-50 and 59. Malformed values will throw `java.util.NoSuchElementException`, resulting in 500 errors instead of proper 400 responses. Use a safe parsing helper that returns `Option` or validates before `.map(withName)`.

<details>
<summary>🤖 Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

```
Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed.

In
`@personalaccesstoken/adapter/src/scala/domino/personalaccesstoken/api/PersonalAccessTokenApiImpl.scala`
around lines 48 - 50, PersonalAccessTokenApiImpl currently calls
PatStatus.withName, PatSortBy.withName and SortOrder.withName directly on query
strings which throws on invalid values; add a small safe parser helper (e.g.,
def safeEnum[T](s: String)(f: String => T): Option[T] = Try(f(s)).toOption or a
withNameOpt wrapper) and replace usages so you call
status.flatMap(safeEnum(PatStatus.withName)),
sortBy.flatMap(safeEnum(PatSortBy.withName)) and
sortOrder.flatMap(safeEnum(SortOrder.withName)) (also update the other
occurrence where those maps are used) so malformed enums return None and can
produce a 400 instead of throwing.
```

</details>

_⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟠 Major_

**Preserve backward compatibility for renamed OIDC keys.**

At **Line 8** and **Line 9**, switching key names without fallback can silently break existing deployments still using `authentication.oidc.pat.clientid/clientsecret` (e.g., defaulting `patClientId` to `"pat-client"` unexpectedly).



<details>
<summary>🔧 Proposed compatibility patch</summary>

```diff
   `@Inject`() def this(config: Configuration) = this(
-    patClientId = config.getOptional[String]("authentication.oidc.pat.clientId").getOrElse("pat-client"),
-    patClientSecret = config.getOptional[String]("authentication.oidc.pat.clientSecret")
+    patClientId = config
+      .getOptional[String]("authentication.oidc.pat.clientId")
+      .orElse(config.getOptional[String]("authentication.oidc.pat.clientid"))
+      .getOrElse("pat-client"),
+    patClientSecret = config
+      .getOptional[String]("authentication.oidc.pat.clientSecret")
+      .orElse(config.getOptional[String]("authentication.oidc.pat.clientsecret"))
   )
```
</details>

<details>
<summary>🤖 Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

```
Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed.

In
`@personalaccesstoken/domain/src/scala/domino/personalaccesstoken/domain/services/PersonalAccessTokenConfig.scala`
around lines 8 - 9, The config lookup for patClientId and patClientSecret
replaced old OIDC key names and can break existing deployments; update the
resolution in PersonalAccessTokenConfig so patClientId uses
config.getOptional[String]("authentication.oidc.pat.clientId") or, if absent,
falls back to the legacy key "authentication.oidc.pat.clientid" before
defaulting to "pat-client", and patClientSecret similarly tries
"authentication.oidc.pat.clientSecret" then the legacy
"authentication.oidc.pat.clientsecret" (preserving the existing optional
semantics) — change the code around the patClientId and patClientSecret
assignments to check the new key first, then the legacy key, then the
default/None as appropriate.
```

</details>

_⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟡 Minor_

**Restore the `userId` path validation on the shared parameter.**

The inline path definitions elsewhere still enforce a 24-hex ObjectId pattern, but this shared component now accepts any string. That weakens generated validation and can push malformed IDs into later runtime parsing errors.

<details>
<summary>💡 Suggested fix</summary>

```diff
     userId:
       name: userId
       description: User Id owner of the personal access tokens.
       in: path
       required: true
       schema:
         type: string
+        pattern: "^[0-9a-f]{24}$"
```
</details>

<details>
<summary>🤖 Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

```
Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed.

In `@personalaccesstoken/public/public-api.yaml` around lines 455 - 461, The
shared path parameter definition for userId has been loosened to accept any
string; restore the ObjectId validation by adding the string pattern regex that
matches 24-hex characters to the shared parameter named "userId" in the
components/parameters section (keep type: string and required: true), matching
the inline path definitions’ pattern (e.g. ^[a-fA-F0-9]{24}$) so generated
validation rejects malformed IDs early.
```

</details>

_⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🔴 Critical_

<details>
<summary>🧩 Analysis chain</summary>

🌐 Web query:

`Does MongoDB accept an empty `$and` array in a `find` filter, or is `{ "$and": [] }` invalid?`

💡 Result:

No, MongoDB does not accept an empty $and array in a find filter. The filter { "$and": [] } is invalid and will cause a parse error such as "$and/$or/$nor must be a nonempty array".

Citations:

- 1: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/65195046/dynamic-or-query-is-empty-gives-error-and-or-nor-must-be-a-nonempty-arra
- 2: http://jira.mongodb.org/browse/SERVER-99098
- 3: https://oneuptime.com/blog/post/2025-12-15-fix-failedtoparse-errors-mongodb/view
- 4: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/34684181/string-parsed-to-json-on-causes-assertionerror-mongoerror-cant-canonicaliz

---

🏁 Script executed:

```shell
cat -n personalaccesstoken/repositories/src/scala/domino/personalaccesstoken/repositories/PersonalAccessTokenMetadataPersisterImpl.scala | sed -n '60,80p'
```

Repository: cerebrotech/domino

Length of output: 1048

---



</details>

**Handle the unfiltered case before emitting `$and`.**

When all filter parameters are None, this builds `{ "$and": [] }`, which MongoDB rejects with "$and/$or/$nor must be a nonempty array". This breaks the admin "list all tokens" path at line 62 unless at least one filter is supplied.

<details>
<summary>Suggested fix</summary>

```diff
   private def buildFilter(userId: Option[DominoId], name: Option[String], status: Option[PatStatus]): BsonDocument = {
     val conditions = List(
       userId.map(id => BsonDocument("userId" -> id.raw)),
       name.map(buildNameFilter),
       status.map(buildStatusFilter),
     ).flatten
-    BsonDocument("$and" -> new BsonArray(conditions.asJava))
+    conditions match {
+      case Nil           => BsonDocument()
+      case single :: Nil => single
+      case _             => BsonDocument("$and" -> new BsonArray(conditions.asJava))
+    }
   }
```
</details>

<!-- suggestion_start -->

<details>
<summary>📝 Committable suggestion</summary>

> ‼️ **IMPORTANT**
> Carefully review the code before committing. Ensure that it accurately replaces the highlighted code, contains no missing lines, and has no issues with indentation. Thoroughly test & benchmark the code to ensure it meets the requirements.

```suggestion
  private def buildFilter(userId: Option[DominoId], name: Option[String], status: Option[PatStatus]): BsonDocument = {
    val conditions = List(
      userId.map(id => BsonDocument("userId" -> id.raw)),
      name.map(buildNameFilter),
      status.map(buildStatusFilter),
    ).flatten
    conditions match {
      case Nil           => BsonDocument()
      case single :: Nil => single
```

```
`@personalaccesstoken/repositories/src/scala/domino/personalaccesstoken/repositories/PersonalAccessTokenMetadataPersisterImpl.scala`
around lines 66 - 73, The buildFilter function currently always emits
BsonDocument("$and" -> BsonArray(...)) which produces an empty $and when userId,
name and status are all None; change buildFilter (the method that composes
```

_⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟠 Major_

<details>
<summary>🧩 Analysis chain</summary>

🌐 Web query:

`In MongoDB, does `$regex` interpret metacharacters in user input as regex syntax? What is the safe way to do a literal case-insensitive substring match?`

💡 Result:

Yes, MongoDB's $regex operator interprets metacharacters in user input as regex syntax. If user input contains regex special characters like ., *, +, ?, ^, $, (, ), [, {, |, etc., they will be treated as regex operators rather than literal characters, potentially causing unintended matches, invalid regex errors, or performance issues. There is no injection risk for query structure (no operator injection), but literal matching requires escaping. The safe way for a literal case-insensitive substring match is to escape all regex metacharacters in the user input before constructing the $regex pattern, then wrap it with .* for substring matching and use the 'i' option. Example in JavaScript/Node.js: function escapeRegex(string) { return string.replace(/[.*+?^${}|[\]\\]/g, '\\$&'); } const userInput = 'user*input?'; const pattern = '.*' + escapeRegex(userInput) + '.*'; db.collection.find({ field: { $regex: pattern, $options: 'i' } }); This ensures metacharacters like * and ? are treated literally. Alternative safer approaches avoiding $regex entirely (recommended for performance and index usage): 1. Use collation with strength 2 for case-insensitive exact or prefix matches (requires MongoDB 3.4+): db.collection.find({ field: userInput }).collation({ locale: 'en', strength: 2 }); Note: collation does not natively support substring (contains) searches; use prefix with /^userInput/i after escaping. 2. Store a lowercase version of the field and compare lowercase: db.collection.find({ field_lower: userInput.toLowerCase }); 3. For full-text substring search, use Atlas Search or text indexes (case-insensitive by default). Official docs confirm $regex uses PCRE2 (since 6.1) and requires proper escaping for literals; case-insensitive via 'i' option.

Citations:

- 1: https://www.mongodb.com/docs/manual/reference/operator/query/regex/
- 2: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/20585136/mongodb-regex-search-security-regarding-user-input
- 3: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/37353139/how-to-escape-reqexp-to-find-documents-in-mongodb
- 4: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/76029346/regex-is-not-working-as-expected-in-mongodb
- 5: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/34758663/how-can-i-work-with-mongodb-regex-with-special-characters-like-plus
- 6: http://www.mongodb.org/docs/manual/core/index-case-insensitive/

---



</details>

**Escape user input before using it in a regex pattern.**

MongoDB's `$regex` operator treats metacharacters in user input as regex syntax. Characters like `.*`, `.`, `?`, `+`, and character classes will alter query semantics and can cause expensive scans or invalid patterns. Use `java.util.regex.Pattern.quote(name)` to escape the input before passing it to `BsonRegularExpression`.

<details>
<summary>Suggested fix</summary>

```diff
-    val regex = new BsonRegularExpression(name, "i")
+    val regex = new BsonRegularExpression(java.util.regex.Pattern.quote(name), "i")
```
</details>

<!-- suggestion_start -->

<details>
<summary>📝 Committable suggestion</summary>

> ‼️ **IMPORTANT**
> Carefully review the code before committing. Ensure that it accurately replaces the highlighted code, contains no missing lines, and has no issues with indentation. Thoroughly test & benchmark the code to ensure it meets the requirements.

```suggestion
  private def buildNameFilter(name: String): BsonDocument = {
    val regex = new BsonRegularExpression(java.util.regex.Pattern.quote(name), "i")
    BsonDocument("$or" -> new BsonArray(List(BsonDocument("name" -> regex), BsonDocument("username" -> regex)).asJava))
```

</details>

<!-- suggestion_end -->

```
`@personalaccesstoken/repositories/src/scala/domino/personalaccesstoken/repositories/PersonalAccessTokenMetadataPersisterImpl.scala`

name to construct a BsonRegularExpression which allows regex metacharacters to
```

_⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟠 Major_

**The status filter/sort logic does not match the computed status model.**

`active` only checks `isValid = true`, so expired-but-not-revoked tokens match both `active` and `expired`. Then `status` sorting on `(isValid, expiresAt)` orders storage fields rather than the exposed computed status, which puts revoked/expired entries ahead of active ones in ascending order. This needs mutually exclusive status predicates and an explicit status rank for sorting.

<details>
<summary>🤖 Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

```
Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed.

In
`@personalaccesstoken/repositories/src/scala/domino/personalaccesstoken/repositories/PersonalAccessTokenMetadataPersisterImpl.scala`
around lines 80 - 97, The status predicates and sort don’t reflect the computed
status model: update buildStatusFilter to make predicates mutually exclusive
(Active => isValid = true AND (no expiresAt OR expiresAt > now), ExpiringSoon =>
isValid = true AND expiresAt > now AND expiresAt <= expiringSoonLimit, Expired
=> isValid = true AND expiresAt <= now, Revoked => isValid = false) so tokens
cannot match multiple statuses; then update buildSortBson so PatSortBy.Status
sorts by an explicit computed status rank (e.g. a numeric statusRank where
Active=0, ExpiringSoon=1, Expired=2, Revoked=3) rather than raw storage fields —
compute statusRank in the query projection/aggregation and use
BsonDocument("statusRank" -> sortOrder, "expiresAt" -> sortOrder) in
buildSortBson (functions: buildStatusFilter, buildSortBson; enums: PatStatus,
PatSortBy).
```

</details>
